content_analysis.research_question: The paper compares the clinical manifestations and mortality of highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) H5Nx outbreaks among different poultry types (laying-type chickens, meat-type chickens, and ducks) and age groups (juvenile vs. adult) in the Netherlands between 2014 and 2022 to improve early warning criteria.
methods: Data collection from 92 HPAI-infected Dutch commercial farms (2014–2022) using NVWA and Royal GD visit reports, production charts, and interviews.
Categorization of poultry by genetic background: Galliformes layer-type, Galliformes meat-type, and Anseriformes (ducks).
Categorization by age: Juvenile (e.g., broilers, laying pullets, meat ducks) vs. Adult (e.g., layers, breeders).
Systematic assessment of clinical signs using a standardized checkbox list covering six organ systems (general, respiratory, nervous/locomotor, mucosal/skin, gastrointestinal, reproductive).
Calculation of Mortality Ratio (MR) comparing mortality on the day before notification to the average of the previous week.
Statistical analysis using Fisher’s exact tests (p < 0.05) to find associations between poultry types, ages, and clinical manifestations.
Temporal analysis comparing two periods: 2014–2018 (H5N8/H5N6) vs. 2020–2022 (H5N8/H5N1).

findings.finding: Outbreak distribution shifted significantly over time, with meat-type poultry cases increasing from 6.3% (2014–2018) to 39.5% (2020–2022).
Clinical signs varied by species: Ducks showed higher rates of nervous/locomotor signs (66.7%) and reproductive tract signs (100%), while chickens showed more mucosal/skin signs (55.0%–67.8%).
Juveniles (chickens and ducks) exhibited neurological and locomotor signs significantly more often than adults (p < 0.001).
A Mortality Ratio (MR) > 3 on the day before notification was more common in chickens (80–82.6%) than in ducks (53.8%).
Mortality typically increased exponentially within the four days prior to notification for most chicken and duck flocks.

figures.description: Bar chart comparing the frequency of clinical signs by organ system across four groups: adult ducks, juvenile ducks, adult chickens, and juvenile chickens.
Log10 scale plot of daily mortality percentages for layer-type poultry, meat-type poultry, and ducks during the 26 days prior to notification.
Log10 scale plot of daily mortality percentages categorized by age group (juvenile vs. adult for both chickens and ducks) prior to notification.
Stacked bar chart showing the proportion of farms with increased, normal, or unknown mortality ratios (MR) one day before notification by poultry group.

tables.description: Provides the raw counts of infected farms per season and poultry subtype.
Summarizes the availability of various data sources used in the study.
Details how specific poultry subtypes were aggregated into larger categories for statistical analysis.
Compares the distribution of infected farms by type and age between the two main study periods.
Statistical summary of clinical signs observed on farms, grouped by production type.
Displays the number and percentage of farms that reached the 'early warning' threshold of MR > 3.
Compares clinical sign frequencies on layer farms between the 2014–2018 and 2020–2022 periods.

limitations: Small sample sizes for certain subgroups, such as adult ducks (n=5) and turkey farms (n=6).
Reliance on passive surveillance, which depends on farmer/veterinarian recognition of signs to trigger notification.
Unavailability of reliable farm production charts for 36 out of 92 farms, limiting mortality ratio calculations.
Intertwining of virus strains, housing systems, and management practices, which makes it difficult to isolate host vs. virus factors.
connections: Builds on the mortality ratio (MR) calculation method established by Gonzales and Elbers (2018).
Utilizes clinical sign categorization and epidemiological framework from Schreuder et al. (2021).
Integrates data with European-wide trends reported by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA).
References IVPI and susceptibility studies by Vreman et al. (2022) and Beerens et al. (2021).

draft_questions.question: What was the specific percentage increase in the distribution of HPAI outbreaks in meat-type poultry when comparing the 2014–2018 period to the 2020–2022 period?
draft_questions.expected_answer: The distribution of HPAI outbreaks in meat-type poultry increased from 6.3% in the 2014–2018 period to 39.5% in the 2020–2022 period.

draft_questions.question: How do the clinical presentations and mortality detection thresholds of HPAI differ between chicken populations (Galliformes) and duck populations (Anseriformes) according to the study?
draft_questions.expected_answer: Ducks are more likely to exhibit nervous/locomotor (66.7%) and reproductive signs (100%), whereas chickens primarily show mucosal/skin signs (55.0–67.8%). Additionally, chickens are more easily detected via mortality monitoring, with over 80% reaching a Mortality Ratio (MR) > 3 before notification, compared to only 53.8% of duck flocks.

draft_questions.question: How did the researchers define and calculate the Mortality Ratio (MR) metric used to assess early warning signals on the infected farms?
draft_questions.expected_answer: The Mortality Ratio (MR) was calculated by comparing the mortality recorded on the day before notification to the average mortality recorded during the previous week. A threshold of MR > 3 was specifically used to identify farms that reached an early warning level of increased mortality.
